Subject: Follow-up: Marketplace private offer & unit-price assumptions

Hi Elena — great to meet earlier today. Quick recap and a couple of asks so procurement has what it needs to evaluate a marketplace private offer (AWS preferred per the call).

Recap (from our discovery):
- Estimated steady-state monthly tokens: 80M - 150M tokens/month (peak bursts up to ~250M).
- Current integration timeline: POC in May, production pilot in June, GA in Q3.
- Sensitive PHI-adjacent workloads; will need Private (VPC) option and SOC2 evidence.

Pricing inputs we discussed / need confirmed:
1) Target unit price (your desired $ per 1M tokens) — you mentioned a target range on the call; can you confirm the exact target or preferred band?
2) Expected committed spend window (monthly commit, 6/12/24 mo) — marketplace private offers require a mapping to a SKU/unit price so we can estimate discounts.
3) Procurement contact & PO cadence — please confirm who will approve private offer and if DocuSign or vendor portal is preferred.

What Redwood can provide for AWS Marketplace PO mapping:
- We can publish a private offer SKU mapped to a $/1M tokens unit price.
- We support tiered unit pricing (e.g., $X per 1M up to 100M, $Y thereafter) and/or multi-month commit mapping.
- For token-count billing we provide monthly usage reports and a Hallmark ledger export that procurement can ingest.

Action items / ask:
- Elena: please confirm the target $/1M token you want us to model (range is fine) and preferred commit term.
- Carlos (procurement): if you’re looping in procurement, can you confirm PO approver and preferred PO template?
- Redwood: we’ll draft an initial unit-price mapping spreadsheet and a PO sample for AWS private offers (attached: unit-price-proposal.xlsx).

I attached a unit-price sketch showing three mapping options: conservative, balanced, and upfront-commit (numbers are illustrative until you confirm the target band).

Available this week for a 30-min pricing alignment with procurement & partner ops: Thu 3/11 10:00–12:00 PT or Fri 3/12 09:00–11:00 PT. What works best?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales – Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0197

title: Clarify: onshore KMS, no-train evidence & response ownership
preview:
From: Marisol Vega <legal@goodhealth.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Romero <alex.romero@goodhealth.ai>
Date: Tue, 09 Mar 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: evidence for no-training/no-derivatives + KMS residency

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Legal and procurement asked for a short list of artifacts so we can clear GoodHealth's procurement checklist. High level asks:

- A clear operational description of how Redwood implements a customer-level "no-training"/no-derivatives commitment (is it a toggle, policy enforcement, architectural separation?).
- Concrete evidence we can deliver to our security reviewers: sample audit log extract showing a request and how it is or isn't used for model updates; a retention/route diagram; and a short template for the evidence pack.
- Confirmation on encryption/key routing: can keys be customer-managed (BYOK) with key material stored in an onshore HSM/KMS (US-only)? If not fully supported, what are the practical mitigations?
- A definitive statement on prompt/response ownership and whether Redwood retains or uses prompts/outputs for any internal experiments or model improvements.

We attached our DPA redlines and a procurement schema (evidence_schema_request.xlsx) for what our reviewers expect. Can you confirm what you can provide and an estimated timeline? If it helps we can schedule a 30min call with security and procurement tomorrow.

Appreciate the quick turnaround — procurement is aiming to close this week.

Best,
Marisol Vega
Head of Legal Operations
GoodHealth.ai

Attachments: GoodHealth_DPA_Redlines_v2.pdf (pdf), evidence_schema_request.xlsx (xlsx)

To: Marisol Vega <legal@goodhealth.ai>
Cc: Alex Romero <alex.romero@goodhealth.ai>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>, Olga Petrov <olga@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 09 Mar 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: evidence for no-training/no-derivatives + KMS residency

Hi Marisol — thanks for the clear list and for sending the redlines.

Quick note on next steps: I've looped in Dev (infrastructure/security) and Olga (legal) to own the evidence pack and DPA comments respectively. We can put together the sample audit extract and an operations note describing the no-train toggle and routing.

Proposed timeline:
- Today: confirm feasibility and list of deliverables (we will outline exactly what will be included in the evidence pack).
- Tomorrow (end of day): security sends a draft evidence extract + architecture summary.
- Thursday: Olga provides precise contract language or an addendum we can sign.

We can do a 30m call tomorrow 10–10:30am PT to walk procurement/security through the artifacts — does that work for your team?

Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0172



From: Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>
To: Marisol Vega <legal@goodhealth.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Romero <alex.romero@goodhealth.ai>, Olga Petrov <olga@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 08:27:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: evidence for no-training/no-derivatives + KMS residency

Hi Marisol / Alex — quick technical summary below and what we'll include in the evidence pack. TL;DR: Redwood supports per-customer enforcement of a model-improvement opt-out, produces audit exports, and supports BYOK with HSM-based key wrapping (with onshore residency options depending on deployment).

1) No-training / model-improvement opt-out (operational):
- Implementation: customer-level configuration flag (tenant policy) at the control plane that disables ingestion of any customer content into model training pipelines. The flag governs downstream ETL and prevents content from being queued to our training repositories.
- Enforcement: there is a gated ingestion service which requires explicit tenancy consent before data is forwarded to any long-term model training store. When the opt-out is set, that forwarding path is disabled at the router layer and enforced by policy at both the serving runtime and ingestion services.
- Isolation for Dedicated/Private: for Dedicated and Private deployments, we can provide an operator-run or customer-operated flow that ensures no data leaves the customer's GPU pool for training.

2) Evidence we will produce (samples included in pack):
- Audit log extract: anonymized log lines showing request id, tenant id, timestamp, operation, and routing decision (e.g., "forwarded_to_training=false"). We'll provide a 10-row CSV example with realistic timestamps and request ids.
- Dataflow diagram: PNG showing runtime, KV/cache behavior, and where the "no-train" gate is.
- Retention settings: table listing default & configurable retention windows for request logs, ephemeral caches, and long-term telemetry.
- Proof of non-use statement: signed ops statement describing that no human-in-the-loop or model training jobs have ingested GoodHealth data.

3) Prompt/response ownership and usage:
- Ownership: customer retains ownership of prompts and outputs per our standard DPA language.
- Operational use: prompts/outputs are not surfaced to model improvement pipelines when the tenant opt-out is enabled. Telemetry (aggregate token counts, latency metrics) may be kept for service operation; raw prompt/response text will be excluded from improvement datasets when opt-out is active.

4) KMS / key residency and BYOK:
- Hosted (standard): we support customer-managed keys (BYOK) using key wrapping—customers can provision keys in their cloud KMS and we wrap session keys for models. However, session metadata and some key-wrapping metadata may transit our control plane (we can supply a detailed SOC2 section on the flows).
- Onshore HSM option: for enterprise Private deployments (VPC or on-prem), we can deploy a KMS/HSM inside the customer's region or integrate with an on-prem HSM (FIPS/HSM validated). This supports US-only residency. For shared-hosted tiers, full key residency for every artifact is constrained — we provide contractual and technical mitigations (encryption in transit/rest + strict access controls).

5) Subprocessors and export controls:
- We'll include the current subprocessors list and highlight any that are used for telemetry/model infra. Exports: we don't export prompt text for model training when opt-out is enabled.

Deliverables (what you'll get tomorrow 17:00 PT):
- 10-row anonymized audit log CSV (request_id, tenant_id, ts, op, forwarded_to_training)
- Single-page dataflow PNG (800x600)
- Short ops statement (PDF)
- KMS/HSM options note (docx)

If you'd like the audit extract to contain specific example fields from your procurement schema, attach the schema and we'll align the columns.

— Dev
VP, Infrastructure Security (Redwood Inference)
dev@redwood.com



From: Olga Petrov <olga@redwood.com>
To: Marisol Vega <legal@goodhealth.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Romero <alex.romero@goodhealth.ai>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 12:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: evidence for no-training/no-derivatives + KMS residency

Marisol — thanks for the redlines. A few pragmatic legal points and proposed language to close quickly:

1) No-training commitment: we can offer a contract addendum that says, in short form: \"Redwood will not use Customer Content for model training, fine-tuning, or evaluation without the Customer's prior written consent. This commitment applies to Redwood and its subprocessors, subject to the list of subprocessors appended to this DPA.\"

2) Evidence scope: procurement often needs both an operational statement and a sample export. We'll provide an "evidence addendum" template that references the audit CSV and ops statement Dev mentioned. That addendum can be appended to the DPA and signed electronically (DocuSign).

3) KMS/BYOK: contractually we can commit to support BYOK for encryption-at-rest keys and to take commercially reasonable steps to enable key residency for Private and Dedicated deployments. For Hosted customers we can provide an HSM-backed wrap but cannot guarantee physical key residency across the entire hosted fleet without a Private deployment. Suggested clause: \"For Dedicated or Private deployments, Redwood will, at Customer's request, deploy or integrate KMS/HSM instances within Customer-selected jurisdictions to meet key residency obligations. For Hosted deployments, Redwood will employ key wrapping and access controls and will cooperate on migration plans to Dedicated/Private deployments where residency is required.\"

4) Prompt/response IP: we accept the customer ownership framing; we'll add explicit language indemnifying against Redwood using prompts/outputs for model improvements when the no-train flag is set. We will ask to keep telemetry/aggregate metrics as described for platform health.

Next steps: I'll produce redline language (short addendum + suggested clause text) by EOD Thursday. If GoodHealth needs a binding SLA or a signed DPA addendum sooner, we can prioritize.

Olga Petrov
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
olga@redwood.com



From: Marisol Vega <legal@goodhealth.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwood.com>, Olga Petrov <olga@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Romero <alex.romero@goodhealth.ai>
Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 16:43:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: evidence for no-training/no-derivatives + KMS residency

Thanks — this is exactly what we needed.

We'll review the sample audit extract tomorrow morning. The procurement team confirmed the 10-row CSV format will work for the reviewers as long as the tenant_id column is present (can be a test tenant id).

Call: 10:00am PT tomorrow works — Alex and I will join with procurement and a technical reviewer. Please send calendar invite.

Marisol

To: Marisol Vega <legal@goodhealth.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>

Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 12:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: evidence for no-training/no-derivatives + KMS residency

Marisol — thanks for the redlines. A few pragmatic legal points and proposed language to close quickly:

1) No-training commitment: we can offer a contract addendum that says, in short form: \"Redwood will not use Customer Content for model training, fine-tuning, or evaluation without the Customer's prior written consent. This commitment applies to Redwood and its subprocessors, subject to the list of subprocessors appended to this DPA.\"

2) Evidence scope: procurement often needs both an operational statement and a sample export. We'll provide an "evidence addendum" template that references the audit CSV and ops statement Dev mentioned. That addendum can be appended to the DPA and signed electronically (DocuSign).

3) KMS/BYOK: contractually we can commit to support BYOK for encryption-at-rest keys and to take commercially reasonable steps to enable key residency for Private and Dedicated deployments. For Hosted customers we can provide an HSM-backed wrap but cannot guarantee physical key residency across the entire hosted fleet without a Private deployment. Suggested clause: \"For Dedicated or Private deployments, Redwood will, at Customer's request, deploy or integrate KMS/HSM instances within Customer-selected jurisdictions to meet key residency obligations. For Hosted deployments, Redwood will employ key wrapping and access controls and will cooperate on migration plans to Dedicated/Private deployments where residency is required.\"

4) Prompt/response IP: we accept the customer ownership framing; we'll add explicit language indemnifying against Redwood using prompts/outputs for model improvements when the no-train flag is set. We will ask to keep telemetry/aggregate metrics as described for platform health.

Next steps: I'll produce redline language (short addendum + suggested clause text) by EOD Thursday. If GoodHealth needs a binding SLA or a signed DPA addendum sooner, we can prioritize.

Subject: Post-call: telemetry export & immutability questions (SOC2 / HIPAA)

Hi Elena —

Thanks again for the time today. Quick recap from our discovery so your security team has a single note:

- Compliance scope: SOC 2 is required; BrightPath also flagged HIPAA applicability for PHI flows and GDPR for EU patient records.
- Main logging asks: admin activity logs (console & console admin API calls), API request/response logs, and configurable export for e-discovery.
- Important: exportability + immutability (WORM/append-only) are gating items for your procurement team.

Appreciate the clear recap. A few clarifying questions from our InfoSec/Compliance reviewers:

1) Retention windows: do you support per-log-type retention (e.g., admin activity 7 years, API logs 1 year)? Is retention configurable per-tenant?
2) Immutability: do you provide an append-only export target (S3 with Object Lock / WORM) or an internal immutable store we can pull from? We need proof points for procurement.
3) Export format: CSV is fine but can you also push JSONL or Parquet? We run analytics directly on Parquet files.
4) Chain of custody: does Redwood add any cryptographic hash/manifest metadata to exported files to verify no tampering?

Also attaching our vendor questionnaire (we've redacted some PHI examples) — can your SIQ response reference exact API endpoints and sample schemas? Our security lead (copied) may ask for a one-hour deep dive with your SE.

Elena — thanks, useful list. Quick answers and next steps below; Karthik will follow with technical specifics.

Short answers:
- Per-log-type retention: yes, tenant-level retention policies are configurable in the Redwood Console. You can set different windows for admin-activity, API request logs, and inference telemetry.
- Immutability: we support export to customer-controlled S3 with Object Lock (compliant with WORM) and we also maintain internal write-once archives for 90 days for immediate investigative needs.
- Export formats: CSV, JSONL, Parquet supported. Exports are schema-stable and include a manifest.
- Chain of custody: export bundles include a SHA256 manifest and a signed metadata file with timestamp and job id.

Next steps:
- Karthik: can you run the export walkthrough and show Parquet export + S3 Object Lock flow?
- Marissa: will annotate the SIQ to call out exact endpoints and attach sample schema definitions.
- Elena/security@brightpathhealth.com: once we get your questionnaire back we will populate and return within 48 hours.

Ben

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Elena Garcia <elena.garcia@brightpathhealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: telemetry export & immutability questions (SOC2 / HIPAA)

Hi Elena / Ben —

Short technical drill-down from the SE side so your security folks have concrete answers ahead of the walkthrough:

1) Admin activity logs (what we include):
   - actor (user_id), actor_display_name, action_type (create, update, revoke_key), object_type, object_id, timestamp_utc, request_id, client_ip, location, change_summary

2) API request logs:
   - method, path, status_code, latency_ms, request_body_sha256 (if enabled), response_size_bytes, timestamp_utc, request_id

3) Export mechanics:
   - Exports run as jobs from Console or via the Admin API (/v1/exports).
   - Output bundles: data.{part}.parquet, manifest.json (sha256 hashes for each part), signed_metadata.json (job_id, started_at, completed_at, exporter_id).
   - Destination options: customer S3 (recommended) with recommended bucket policy + Object Lock enabled; Azure Blob immutability also supported.

4) Immutability / chain of custody options:
   - Recommend: export to customer S3 with Object Lock + Glacier Vault Lock for long-term WORM.
   - We can additionally write a signed manifest to a separate retention bucket for extra proof.

5) Retention config:
   - Console supports per-log-type TTLs; API also allows retention override per-export job for legal holds.

6) Sample manifest snippet (example):
   {"job_id":"exp-7a2f","parts":[{"file":"data.0001.parquet","sha256":"3b8f..."}],"started_at":"2027-03-09T18:00:12Z","completed_at":"2027-03-09T18:01:03Z","exporter":"redwood-exporter-02"}

If helpful, I can include a short Postman collection showing /v1/exports and a script to validate manifests (sha256 checks). I can also walk through enabling Object Lock on a test S3 bucket during the walkthrough.

Available times: Wed 10:00am PT (confirmed) — I'll send a calendar invite once you confirm.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
karthik_iyer@redwood.com | +1 650-555-0202

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Elena Garcia <elena.garcia@brightpathhealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: telemetry export & immutability questions (SOC2 / HIPAA)

Hi Elena / Ben —

Short technical drill-down from the SE side so your security folks have concrete answers ahead of the walkthrough:

1) Admin activity logs (what we include):
   - actor (user_id), actor_display_name, action_type (create, update, revoke_key), object_type, object_id, timestamp_utc, request_id, client_ip, location, change_summary

2) API request logs:
   - method, path, status_code, latency_ms, request_body_sha256 (if enabled), response_size_bytes, timestamp_utc, request_id

3) Export mechanics:
   - Exports run as jobs from Console or via the Admin API (/v1/exports).
   - Output bundles: data.{part}.parquet, manifest.json (sha256 hashes for each part), signed_metadata.json (job_id, started_at, completed_at, exporter_id).
   - Destination options: customer S3 (recommended) with recommended bucket policy + Object Lock enabled; Azure Blob immutability also supported.

4) Immutability / chain of custody options:
   - Recommend: export to customer S3 with Object Lock + Glacier Vault Lock for long-term WORM.
   - We can additionally write a signed manifest to a separate retention bucket for extra proof.

5) Retention config:
   - Console supports per-log-type TTLs; API also allows retention override per-export job for legal holds.

6) Sample manifest snippet (example):
   {"job_id":"exp-7a2f","parts":[{"file":"data.0001.parquet","sha256":"3b8f..."}],"started_at":"2027-03-09T18:00:12Z","completed_at":"2027-03-09T18:01:03Z","exporter":"redwood-exporter-02"}

If helpful, I can include a short Postman collection showing /v1/exports and a script to validate manifests (sha256 checks). I can also walk through enabling Object Lock on a test S3 bucket during the walkthrough.

Available times: Wed 10:00am PT (confirmed) — I'll send a calendar invite once you confirm.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

title: Integration recap: SDK/auth/routing + dedicated sizing follow-ups
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finoptics.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 9 Mar 2027 15:10:00 -0700
Subject: Integration recap: SDK/auth/routing + dedicated sizing follow-ups

Hi Samantha / Carlos —

Thanks again for the demo and the deep-dive today. Quick recap and the open items we agreed to track: 

1) Integration surface
- SDK: you want language SDKs (Python + Node) and an examples repo for structured output/tool-calling patterns.
- Auth: customer-managed API keys for test, plus planned KMS integration for production.
- Routing: prefer region-first routing (us-east primary) with fallback to a lower-cost variant for non-critical traffic.

2) Model + features
- Structured output + tool calling: primary use for document extraction and follow-up actions.
- Embeddings + reranker: embeddings for semantic search; we discussed approximate candidate sizes (1k-5k docs initially).

3) Dedicated capacity sizing (ballpark asks from your Ops team)
- Steady-state: ~60 concurrent sessions, average 1.2k tokens/request, expected 20 reqs/s peak for classification + structured output workload.
- Latency SLO desire: p95 < 400ms for short completions; batchable longer jobs can be async.
- Autoscale: want floor/ceiling and burst to public pool when backpressure happens.

Open technical items (action owners):
- (Karthik) confirm production auth/KMS pattern and whether FinOptics needs HSM-backed keys.
- (Marissa) provide SDK onboarding repo + sample structured-output templates.
- (Ben) propose dedicated sizing + queueing plan and confirm concurrency limits.

I've attached the traffic snapshot you sent and a short SDK/auth playbook we maintain internally. Propose we sync next Tuesday 10am PT to lock down sizing. If that works, I'll send a calendar invite.

Cheers,
Ben

--
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0191


From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finoptics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Carlos Mendez <carlos.mendez@finoptics.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 9 Mar 2027 16:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Integration recap: SDK/auth/routing + dedicated sizing follow-ups

Thanks Ben — this is helpful and accurate. A couple clarifications from our side: 

- The 20 reqs/s number is peak for our interactive search flow (reranker + structured output). The bulk of the traffic is short completions (~200–400 tokens) but we have ~15% of requests that include longer tool-calls (1k–1.5k tokens).
- Concurrency: our current app-level concurrency target is 50 active LLM requests; we anticipate growth to ~120 over 6 months if the pilot proves out.
- For auth: we can use customer-managed keys in KMS but would prefer a flow that avoids sharing raw keys (token exchange or short-lived certs). We'll surface security requirements in the questionnaire, but HSM isn't required initially.

We'll review the SDK playbook and the snapshot you attached. Tuesday 10am PT works for us.

Samantha
Director, Product Engineering — FinOptics
Samantha.lee@finoptics.com

> On Tue, Mar 9, 2027 at 3:10 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> > (original message)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finoptics.com>, Carlos Mendez <carlos.mendez@finoptics.com>
Date: Wed, 10 Mar 2027 01:00:00 -0000
Subject: Re: Integration recap: SDK/auth/routing + dedicated sizing follow-ups

Looping in some technical constraints we should call out before the Tue call: 

- Concurrency/capacity: for dedicated pools we typically size by sustained tokens/sec and max concurrent KV cache sessions. Given their steady-state numbers (20 req/s * avg 600 tokens) ~= 12k tokens/s sustained; with peaks factoring 1.5x -> ~18k tokens/s. That suggests a 2-3 GPU dedicated pool depending on model choice and quantization.
- Queues & backpressure: recommend a dual-queue approach — low-latency queue (strict SLO, limited concurrency) and a batch queue for tool-calls/long runs. If low-latency queue fills, routes spill to batch queue or fall back to hosted lower-cost variant.
- Autoscale policy: min nodes = pool for guaranteed throughput (sustained), scale up when token/sec > 70% for 60s (cooldown), scale down when < 40% for 5m. Burst to public pool should be opt-in (governed by cost-sharing).
- Auth: we'll draft a short-lived token exchange flow (OIDC-backed) so FinOptics doesn't have to hand us long-lived keys.

I'll prepare a short draft SLO + capacity calc doc for Tuesday.

—Karthik
Staff Engineer, Serving Runtime

> On Tue, Mar 9, 2027 at 3:10 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> > (original message)

2) FinOptics: provide a sample of 200 anonymized request payloads (preferred) or an aggregated distribution if payloads aren't shareable. This lets us calibrate batching benefits and queue thresholds.

If we don't get the anonymized samples, we'll run two sizing scenarios: conservative (no batching, higher concurrency) and optimistic (prefix caching + batching savings ~30%).

Calendar invite sent for Tue 10am PT; if anything changes ping me and we'll reschedule.

Best,
Ben

title: Structured Prepayment Release Schedule & VAT Treatment Checklist (FarisTech renewal)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Anna Müller <anna.mueller@faristech.de>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 05 Mar 2027 08:12:00 -0700
Subject: Structured prepayment release schedule & VAT checklist for renewal

Hi Anna —

Following our renewal call this morning, I wanted to put a simple proposed approach for the committed spend prepay and the drawdown schedule. High level:

- Redwood issues a single annual prepay invoice for the committed amount (USD or EUR as you prefer).
- We establish four milestone release buckets (Q2/Q3/Q4/Q1) for drawdowns; each bucket can be consumed monthly by your engineers and we will allocate credits against usage.
- Finance ops: we can either issue periodic credit memos as drawdowns happen or post monthly consumption invoices tied to the prepay ledger.
- VAT: we need to confirm whether FarisTech wants net EUR invoices (you are in DE) or if we keep USD invoices and have you apply reverse-charge.

I attached a tentative schedule (Redwood_Prepay_Schedule_Q2-2027.xlsx) and a short VAT mapping doc (VAT_Mapping_EU.pdf). Can you confirm:
1) preferred billing currency (EUR vs USD)
2) whether you require invoices net of VAT with your VAT ID on the invoice, or if reverse-charge is preferred on your side
3) PO/reference to include on the invoice

If you confirm net EUR and require supplier registration on our side, we’ll loop in tax to confirm next steps — otherwise we’ll include the reverse-charge language on the invoice and include your VAT ID.

Next steps from Redwood: once you confirm the above we’ll produce a draft invoice + credit schedule for sign-off.

Thanks —

Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0124


From: Anna Müller <anna.mueller@faristech.de>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: finance@faristech.de, Thomas Green <t.green@faristech.de>
Date: Fri, 05 Mar 2027 16:47:22 +0100
Subject: Re: Structured prepayment release schedule & VAT checklist for renewal

HI Ben —

Thanks for the clear summary. A few quick answers:

- Currency: we prefer EUR (internal ledger and treasury are EUR).
- VAT: FarisTech GmbH will take reverse-charge where possible, but our accounting team prefers supplier invoices that show net amounts and a clear reference to reverse-charge rules. Our VAT ID: DE123456789.
- PO: please reference PO FTG-2027-450 when issuing.

We would like the invoice split into four release milestones (one per quarter) for internal budget allocations. If you can provide a version with 1) annual invoice + amortization schedule OR 2) four milestone invoices (we prefer the latter), that would be best.

I’ve copied our AP contact (finance@faristech.de) and Thomas for payment timing. Can you also provide a supplier form (standard vendor form) we can attach to our records?

Best,
Anna Müller
Head of Procurement | FarisTech GmbH
anna.mueller@faristech.de | +49 30 555 2345

Date: Sat, 06 Mar 2027 09:03:10 -0700
Subject: Re: Structured prepayment release schedule & VAT checklist for renewal

Ben / team —

Thanks — a few accounting notes before we produce paperwork:

- If FarisTech wants net EUR invoices we need to confirm whether Redwood should register for VAT in DE. That creates additional compliance overhead (monthly filings). Alternative: Redwood invoices in EUR with a reverse-charge line and leaves VAT handling to the buyer — simpler from our side. We need Anna to confirm their preference and confirm they will accept reverse-charge wording on our invoice.
- For ledgering: our ops prefer to issue a single invoice referencing the annual committed amount, but with four invoice numbers for milestone recognition so AP can post to their periods. That covers both desires: 1 annual commitment on our books, 4 recognized invoice docs on theirs.
- Please ask for the vendor/supplier form (they requested it). If FarisTech insists on four independent invoices (not just milestone refs) we should ensure DocuSign signatures will be in place for each invoice to satisfy audit.

I'll prepare two templates: (A) \"Annual invoice with four milestone refs\" and (B) \"Four discrete invoices (Q invoicing)\" — we'll send both to Anna for selection. Marcus: can you confirm the ledger/bucket names and GL mapping so we can produce template fields?

Lauren Bishop
Head of Revenue Operations | Redwood Inference
lauren.bishop@redwood.ai


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>

Date: Mon, 08 Mar 2027 11:25:42 -0700
Subject: Re: Structured prepayment release schedule & VAT checklist for renewal

Quick ledger note —

We should create distinct ledger buckets for the contract: FTG-2027-ANNUAL-01_Q2, _Q3, _Q4, _Q1. Each bucket will hold the allocated prepay credits. When consumption occurs we debit the bucket and post a monthly usage memo. For accounting close: if Anna wants four invoices that is fine — we will map each invoice to the corresponding bucket. If we do one annual invoice with four refs we will still post revenue recognition per bucket on our side.

DocuSign / Invoicing workflow: we should route the initial invoice envelope to Anna and their AP contact; for multiple invoices we can prepare one envelope with four documents to speed signature. Also: include the following on each invoice: PO FTG-2027-450, FarisTech VAT ID DE123456789, and the phrase: \"Reverse charge — Article 196 (intra-EU supply)\" if they accept reverse-charge.

Attachments: I’ve dropped a sample invoice template and the suggested GL mapping into the shared Drive (link will be sent by Ops).

Marcus Lin

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Subject: Physical deployment — acceptance criteria, patch cadence & spare parts (post-call)

Hi Karen — great to speak earlier. Thanks for walking us through Helix's constraints and why an air-gapped, on-prem/private deployment is top of the shortlist. Per our call, I wanted to capture the open items and next steps so we can keep momentum. Attaching the HW requirements doc we referenced and a short SLA SKU matrix for maintenance (see attachments).

Recap (agreed):
- Target: private on-prem appliance in secure lab (no inbound internet).
- Priority requirements: offline upgrades, formal maintenance windows, replaceable/spare GPU modules, and signed change control for all upgrades.
- Timeline: target production pilot in Q2; final timeline depends on procurement and acceptance criteria sign-off.

Open questions from your team (copied below) — I've added our current answers inline where we can.

1) Upgrade flow & rollback: Our on-prem package supports staged offline upgrades via signed bundle you transfer over SFTP/USB. We provide a tested rollback image and a runbook. If you want, Karthik can walk through the step-by-step process next week.

2) Patch cadence & emergency fixes: Standard patch cadence is quarterly for non-critical updates; critical security patches are handled as emergency patches with an agreed after-hours window and documented approval route.

3) Spare parts & replacement SLA: We recommend at minimum 2 GPU spare modules per site for N+1. We can provide spares as part of a dedicated support SKU (see Maintenance_SLA_SKU.csv).

4) Remote diagnostics & telemetry: For air-gapped installs we offer an on-site diagnostics agent that writes encrypted logs to a customer-controlled medium. Remote live debugging is available via partner-assisted secure jump host under controlled approval.

Next steps / proposed owners:
- Ben: Share HW requirements (attached) and draft acceptance checklist (today).
- Karthik: Schedule upgrade walk-through for Helix ops (propose Thu 3/11 or Fri 3/12).
- Monica: Prepare support SKU options and spare-parts proposal (by 3/10).
- Karen: Confirm preferred walkthrough date and any additional acceptance criteria items.

Attachments: Redwood_Private_Deploy_HW_REQS.pdf (hardware list & site prep) | Maintenance_SLA_SKU.csv

Thanks — will follow up if I don't hear back on proposed dates.

--
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0147

Thanks Ben — helpful recap and nice to have the attachments. A few clarifying questions from our site ops and security team after reviewing the HW list: 

1) For the offline upgrade bundles you mentioned, can you confirm the maximum outage window expected for a minor vs major upgrade? Our lab windows are typically 4 hours (minor) and 12 hours (major) — we need to know if Redwood fits.

2) The rollback image: how long to validate and apply in an emergency? We need a documented RTO for a failed upgrade scenario.

3) Spare parts logistics: can Redwood hold spares in our country (we're in US East) or will shipping from Redwood's logistics center be required? Lead times are critical for us.

4) Patching approvals: we require change control with a customer-signed RFC in advance for non-emergency changes. Will Redwood integrate with our Jira RFC process or accept signed PDFs?

5) Can you provide a redacted example of the runbook steps for the upgrade so we can share with our engineering manager before committing?

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>

Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 09 Mar 2027 08:14:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Physical deployment — acceptance criteria, patch cadence & spare parts (post-call)

Karen — great questions. Answers below (summary first, then attachments & proposed next steps).

Summary answers:
- Typical outage windows: minor upgrades (config/patch) target <2 hours; major upgrades (model weights + runtime changes) typically require 6-10 hours in our on-prem runs; we can scope to meet your 4/12 window depending on change complexity and pre-staging.
- Rollback RTO: validated rollback apply time is ~45–90 minutes; full validation to confirm system health is typically 2–4 hours depending on test suite depth.
- Spares: we can colocate a spare inventory in US East via our logistics partner (recommended) to meet short lead times; Monica will provide SKU-based pricing and lead times in the support proposal.
- Change control: we can accept signed PDFs for RFC, or integrate with Jira via a webhook that posts an RFC record; implementation requires a short integration task (auth token exchange).
- Runbook sample: attached a redacted upgrade runbook excerpt (inline below) and a short QA checklist we run in a lab before packaging a bundle.

Inline runbook excerpt (redacted):
- Pre-stage: verify checksums on bundle -> verify spare capacity on node -> snapshot KV cache -> notify stakeholders.
- Apply: enable maintenance mode -> stop non-critical daemons -> install runtime bundle -> apply DB/migration if required -> load model weights -> start services.
- Validate: smoke tests (latency < SLA, sample prompts run), KV cache integrity check, end-to-end inference test against acceptance prompt set.
- Rollback: trigger rollback image -> restore KV snapshot -> re-run smoke tests -> notify stakeholders and open incident until validated.

Attachments included: runbook excerpt (embedded in HW PDF), Maintenance_SLA_SKU.csv (support levels and spare-part options). I'll put a Drive folder link with a sample test report shortly.

Proposed next steps:
- Thu 2027-03-11 10:00–11:00 PT: upgrade walk-through (Karthik to lead). Can you confirm?
- Monica to send spare-parts & logistics proposal by 3/10.
- If walkthrough looks good, draft a mutual acceptance checklist for the pilot (Ben to send).

If that schedule works I’ll send a calendar invite with a short agenda and Karthik's environment checklist.

Thanks — talk soon.

--
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com

title: BridgeCore discovery — model fit & safety follow-up
preview:
From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Tom Langston <tom.langston@bridgecore.ai>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 7 Mar 2027 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: BridgeCore discovery — model fit & safety follow-up

Tom — thanks for the time today. Quick written recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment. Short version: you’re evaluating LLMs to power multi-channel customer support triage and a second-screen agent for financial advice questions (transactional, regulated domain). Key constraints you mentioned: low-latency first-response (p95 < 200ms for common queries), strong refusal behavior on regulated advice, and data residency for EU customers in later phases.

Call highlights / observed priorities:
- Primary use case: automated triage + suggested next steps for agents (not autonomous money movement).
- Safety/regulatory: must refuse or escalate on requests that resemble financial planning or investment advice; PII redaction required for certain regions.
- Volume: 10–20k end-user requests/day in pilot; expect growth to 100k/day in 6–9 months.
- Integration: want a hosted API first, but interested in Dedicated/Private for Production.

Initial model-fit recommendations (based on your latency/cost needs & safety sensitivity):
1) rw-13b-opt (4-bit): best cost/latency balance for triage where refusals are simple taxonomy checks. Expected median latency ~90–120ms (tokenized request), baseline TTR throughput for our Dedicated pools.
2) rw-30b-qe (8-bit quant): better at nuanced refusal and policy-following for edge cases — slightly higher cost but lower refusal failure (see attached eval summary).
3) Fallback policy: route ambiguous/high-risk prompts to a stricter safety variant + canary human-in-loop queue.

Safety / refusal plan we propose:
- Run the attached prompt set (bridgecore_prompt_set_v1.txt) through both models.
- Evaluate refusal precision/recall on curated sensitive prompts and in-distribution complaint logs (anonymized).
- Add guardrail layer: normalized prompt pre-checks (PII detection + taxonomy-based escalation) + soft refusals with deterministic escalation tags.

Deliverables I’ll share after this email:
- BridgeCore eval PDF (summary + metrics) — attached.
- Prompt set used for safety/regression checks — attached.
- Eval notebook and raw results in the shared Drive link below.

Proposed next steps (suggested timeline):
1) We’ll run a focused pilot evaluation on your sample dataset (you can upload anonymized logs to the shared folder) — target: 3 business days for initial runs.
2) Technical deep-dive: 60-minute session (runtime + routing + safety) — we suggested slots on Mar 11 below.
3) Pilot mutual action plan (MMP): we’ll populate a draft MMP with success metrics (latency, refusal F1, cost target) and get your signoff.

Quick asks from your side:
- Share a redacted sample of 500–1,000 typical support prompts (drop to Drive).
- Confirm whether EU data residency is a hard requirement for pilot or only for GA.

If that all matches, I’ll queue the eval runs this afternoon and you’ll have the first summary within 72 hours.

Thanks —
Chloe Martin
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0119

Attachments: bridgecore_prompt_set_v1.txt, bridgecore_eval_report_q1.pdf


From: Tom Langston <tom.langston@bridgecore.ai>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Singh <psingh@bridgecore.ai>
Date: Tue, 8 Mar 2027 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: BridgeCore discovery — model fit & safety follow-up

Thanks Chloe — appreciate the clear recap. Two quick clarifications before we drop data into Drive:

1) Can you share the concrete bench numbers you referenced for hallucination/refusal? Specifically: refusal precision, refusal recall, and a short hallucination rate on our approximate domain (financial Q&A). A ballpark cost per 1k requests for rw-13b-opt and rw-30b-qe would also be helpful.

2) Our legal team will want to see SOC2 and data flow diagrams for the hosted option. We may loop them in — Priya is on cc. For the pilot, EU residency is not required but will be required for GA.

Also curious if Redwood can run a small bespoke safety sweep against a 500-sample set we can provide (anonymized) as part of the initial evaluation. Priya — can you confirm we can anonymize logs and share them?

Tom
Head of Product — BridgeCore


From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Tom Langston <tom.langston@bridgecore.ai>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 8 Mar 2027 16:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: BridgeCore discovery — model fit & safety follow-up

On Tue, Mar 8, 2027 at 9:03 AM, Tom Langston <tom.langston@bridgecore.ai> wrote:
> Thanks Chloe — appreciate the clear recap. Two quick clarifications before we drop data into Drive:
>
> 1) Can you share the concrete bench numbers you referenced for hallucination/refusal? Specifically: refusal precision, refusal recall, and a short hallucination rate on our approximate domain (financial Q&A). A ballpark cost per 1k requests for rw-13b-opt and rw-30b-qe would also be helpful.

Thanks — good questions. Quick summary from the pilot eval we ran against the seed prompt set (attached) and a 2k-sample financial FAQ corpus that approximates your domain: all numbers are initial — we’ll run the sweep on your anonymized 500 samples once you upload.

Bench summary (attached full report):
- rw-13b-opt (4-bit): refusal precision 0.88, refusal recall 0.71, hallucination rate (assertive factual errors) ~6.8% on the sample corpus. Median latency 110ms. Estimated hosted cost (baseline) ~ $12–18 per 1k requests (depends on token length / streaming).
- rw-30b-qe (8-bit): refusal precision 0.94, refusal recall 0.83, hallucination rate ~3.1%. Median latency ~200–240ms. Estimated hosted cost ~ $38–50 per 1k requests.

Notes: the bigger model shows materially better refusal recall on ambiguous policy questions — hence our fallback recommendation for edge cases. We can tune rw-13b prompts and add a lightweight classifier to boost recall if cost/latency are tighter.

Safety/regression checks we run by default for pilots:
- Refusal behavior across: investment advice, account takeover attempts, tax/legal disclaimers.
- PII detection and automated redaction heuristics for tokens matching account numbers / SSN patterns.
- Prompt-injection and jailbreak probes (we include a library of ~400 adversarial probes).

Where to find things:
- Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2bBridgeCoreEval (evaluation PDF + raw CSVs).
- Eval notebook (run in Redwood sandbox): https://app.redwood.ai/evals/BRG-2027-041/notebook
- HubSpot deal record: https://hubspot.com/deal/BRG-2027-041

Next steps we can take immediately:
- We’ll run the bespoke sweep on your 500 anonymized samples within 48 hours of upload.
- Schedule the 60-min technical deep-dive — available slots: Mar 11 (10:00–11:00 PT) or Mar 12 (13:00–14:00 PT). Please pick one.
- I’ll drop a draft MMP into the Drive folder for you to comment on.

If Priya confirms data share, we’ll start the eval runs today.

—
Chloe Martin
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

Cc: Priya Singh <psingh@bridgecore.ai>, Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.com>

Subject: Re: BridgeCore discovery — model fit & safety follow-up

Perfect — thanks Chloe. Priya confirms she’ll scrub the 500-sample set and upload by EOD. We’ll take the Mar 11 10:00–11:00 PT slot for the technical deep-dive.

A couple additional asks for the session: can you include someone from runtime (latency/throughput) and a short slide on Dedicated vs Private tradeoffs for EU residency? Also please add a one-page cost estimate for a 3-month pilot (20k/day) vs an estimated GA profile (100k/day).

If you can put the MMP draft in Drive now, I’ll start populating success metrics on our side.

Tom

- Integration: want a hosted API first, but interested in Dedicated/Private for Production.

Initial model-fit recommendations (based on your latency/cost needs & safety sensitivity):
1) rw-13b-opt (4-bit): best cost/latency balance for triage where refusals are simple taxonomy checks. Expected median latency ~90–120ms (tokenized request), baseline TTR throughput for our Dedicated pools.
2) rw-30b-qe (8-bit quant): better at nuanced refusal and policy-following for edge cases — slightly higher cost but lower refusal failure (see attached eval summary).
3) Fallback policy: route ambiguous/high-risk prompts to a stricter safety variant + canary human-in-loop queue.

Safety / refusal plan we propose:
- Run the attached prompt set (bridgecore_prompt_set_v1.txt) through both models.
- Evaluate refusal precision/recall on curated sensitive prompts and in-distribution complaint logs (anonymized).
- Add guardrail layer: normalized prompt pre-checks (PII detection + taxonomy-based escalation) + soft refusals with deterministic escalation tags.

Deliverables I’ll share after this email:
- BridgeCore eval PDF (summary + metrics) — attached.
- Prompt set used for safety/regression checks — attached.
- Eval notebook and raw results in the shared Drive link below.

Proposed next steps (suggested timeline):
1) We’ll run a focused pilot evaluation on your sample dataset (you can upload anonymized logs to the shared folder) — target: 3 business days for initial runs.
2) Technical deep-dive: 60-minute session (runtime + routing + safety) — we suggested slots on Mar 11 below.
3) Pilot mutual action plan (MMP): we’ll populate a draft MMP with success metrics (latency, refusal F1, cost target) and get your signoff.

Quick asks from your side:
- Share a redacted sample of 500–1,000 typical support prompts (drop to Drive).
- Confirm whether EU data residency is a hard requirement for pilot or only for GA.

If that all matches, I’ll queue the eval runs this afternoon and you’ll have the first summary within 72 hours.

Thanks —
Chloe Martin
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0119

Attachments: bridgecore_prompt_set_v1.txt, bridgecore_eval_report_q1.pdf

On Tue, Mar 8, 2027 at 9:03 AM, Tom Langston <tom.langston@bridgecore.ai> wrote:
> Thanks Chloe — appreciate the clear recap. Two quick clarifications before we drop data into Drive:
>
> 1) Can you share the concrete bench numbers you referenced for hallucination/refusal? Specifically: refusal precision, refusal recall, and a short hallucination rate on our approximate domain (financial Q&A). A ballpark cost per 1k requests for rw-13b-opt and rw-30b-qe would also be helpful.

Thanks — good questions. Quick summary from the pilot eval we ran against the seed prompt set (attached) and a 2k-sample financial FAQ corpus that approximates your domain: all numbers are initial — we’ll run the sweep on your anonymized 500 samples once you upload.

Bench summary (attached full report):
- rw-13b-opt (4-bit): refusal precision 0.88, refusal recall 0.71, hallucination rate (assertive factual errors) ~6.8% on the sample corpus. Median latency 110ms. Estimated hosted cost (baseline) ~ $12–18 per 1k requests (depends on token length / streaming).
- rw-30b-qe (8-bit): refusal precision 0.94, refusal recall 0.83, hallucination rate ~3.1%. Median latency ~200–240ms. Estimated hosted cost ~ $38–50 per 1k requests.

Notes: the bigger model shows materially better refusal recall on ambiguous policy questions — hence our fallback recommendation for edge cases. We can tune rw-13b prompts and add a lightweight classifier to boost recall if cost/latency are tighter.

Safety/regression checks we run by default for pilots:
- Refusal behavior across: investment advice, account takeover attempts, tax/legal disclaimers.
- PII detection and automated redaction heuristics for tokens matching account numbers / SSN patterns.
- Prompt-injection and jailbreak probes (we include a library of ~400 adversarial probes).

Where to find things:
- Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2bBridgeCoreEval (evaluation PDF + raw CSVs).
- Eval notebook (run in Redwood sandbox): https://app.redwood.ai/evals/BRG-2027-041/notebook
- HubSpot deal record: https://hubspot.com/deal/BRG-2027-041

Next steps we can take immediately:
- We’ll run the bespoke sweep on your 500 anonymized samples within 48 hours of upload.
- Schedule the 60-min technical deep-dive — available slots: Mar 11 (10:00–11:00 PT) or Mar 12 (13:00–14:00 PT). Please pick one.
- I’ll drop a draft MMP into the Drive folder for you to comment on.

If Priya confirms data share, we’ll start the eval runs today.

Chloe Martin

title: Clarify commit curve vs. volume blend for per-token tiers
preview:
From: Priya Rao <priya@clearlinehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 5 Mar 2027 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Questions on your per-token tiers

Ben — thanks for the proposal you shared earlier. The procurement team has a couple of open items before we can move to legal:

1) We want to understand whether the discounts on your per-token ladder are marginal (only applied to tokens in each band) or blended (the lower blended effective rate applied to all consumption once a breakpoint is crossed). Which pricing approach did you assume in your scenarios?

2) Related: how do true-ups work if our monthly usage spikes above a committed monthly baseline but annual commit is on track? Any mid-quarter reconciliations or invoice credits?

3) Can you provide a short worked example (with numbers) for both marginal and blended for our proposed commit curve? That will help our finance team model cashflows.

Attaching our standard PO template for reference. Can we get a one-pager that shows both calculations by EOD Monday?

Thanks —
Priya Rao
Head of Procurement
Clearline Health

(Attachment: Clearline_PO_Template.pdf)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya@clearlinehealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 5 Mar 2027 10:37:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Questions on your per-token tiers

Priya — thanks for the clear questions. I put together two short scenarios and attached a simple spreadsheet (Proposed_Pricing_Scenarios.xlsx) that walks the math. High level answers first so your team can move quickly: 

- We can support both billing conventions. Historically most of our enterprise customers prefer marginal for visibility into unit economics, but we offer blended if you prefer simpler cash modeling.
- True-up: our standard contract uses monthly metering with an annual true-up. If monthly usage exceeds the committed monthly baseline, we prorate at the applicable marginal band for that month and reflect any overages on the next invoice; at year-end we true-up against the committed annual volume and apply credits/charges per the agreement's true-up table. We can add a mid-quarter true-up clause (optional) if you want a predictable reconciliation cadence.

Worked examples (see sheet 'Worked Examples'):
- Marginal: Commit 600M tokens/yr (50M/mo). Breakpoints: 0-10M @ $0.00008, next 40M @ $0.00006, 50M+ @ $0.00005. If in June you use 80M tokens, you pay 10M*$0.00008 + 40M*$0.00006 + 30M*$0.00005 = $8 + $24 + $15 = $47 for June.
- Blended: Using same breakpoints, once you cross the 50M/mo breakpoint (i.e., 50M committed trigger), blended rate might be, e.g., $0.000055 on all 80M => $4.4. Blended gives a lower-looking per-token amount but increases cost unpredictability on lower months.

Operational notes from our side: batching and caching behavior still applies (billed tokens measured after prefix cache deductions). Any quantization/accelerator discounts are additive and applied post-break calculation per the SOW.

I'll sync with Lauren and Sean on the contract language we'll propose for the true-up cadence and the exact invoice mechanics. Call to discuss Monday morning?

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Lead, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

(Attachment: Proposed_Pricing_Scenarios.xlsx)

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 5 Mar 2027 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Re: Questions on your per-token tiers

Looping ops and billing here — a few practical constraints to flag for the contract draft: 

- If Clearline wants blended rates, legal will need explicit language on when the blended rate is triggered (month-level crossing vs. rolling 3-mo average). We typically see disputes with month-level blenders because seasonality can create surprising credits/charges. Recommend a 3-month rolling trigger if they care about smoothing.

- For marginal true-ups, we need to confirm what token definition they use (prompt+completion vs. completion-only for embedding/semantic calls). That matters for the band math.

- SLA crediting: if they're asking for a committed latency SLA tied to Dedicated routing, make sure we separate compute-tier credits from billing true-ups. Sean — can you check if there's any regional tax handling for their HQ?

I'll draft language for both options and include an optional mid-quarter reconciliation clause as Ben suggested.

-Lauren
Director, Product Partnerships
Redwood Inference

[Quoted text]
> From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
> Date: Fri, 5 Mar 2027 10:37:00 -0700
> Subject: Re: Questions on your per-token tiers
>
> Priya — thanks for the clear questions...

From: Priya Rao <priya@clearlinehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Clearline Health Procurement <procurement@clearlinehealth.com>
Date: Fri, 5 Mar 2027 13:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Questions on your per-token tiers

Thanks, Ben and Lauren — that helps. A couple quick follow-ups from procurement: 

- Can you show the blended example using a 3-month rolling average trigger (as Lauren suggested)?
- We often have month-to-month spikes due to seasonal campaigns; would Redwood consider a soft cap where we get a one-time smoothing credit for a single month spike per quarter (no more than 2 per year)? If so, how would that show on the invoice?

If you can add those two lines to the spreadsheet and return, we can move to legal review. Also proposing a 30-minute call next Tue to finalize — does 10am PT work?

Best,
Priya

(Attachment: none)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Priya Rao <priya@clearlinehealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 5 Mar 2027 17:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Questions on your per-token tiers — next steps

Priya — appreciate the quick turn. I've updated the spreadsheet: new tab '3mo_blend_trigger' shows a blended-rate calculation that only takes effect if the trailing 3-month usage average crosses the breakpoint. Example included where months = [40M, 120M, 80M] => trailing avg = 80M, which triggers the blended band and applies blended price across the trailing months per the optional smoothing clause.

Regarding the soft cap: we can include a Quarterly Spike Smoothing credit: one-month spike per quarter (up to 50% above committed monthly baseline) eligible for a credit equal to the delta calculated at the marginal rate vs. blended (so you get the smaller charge of the two for that month). We'll mark that as optional in the SOW so finance can opt in. On the invoice it will show as an adjustment line item referencing the month and the credit ID.

Notes and next steps:
- I created a HubSpot deal with the pricing scenarios + one-pager (link below).
- Proposed next call: Tue 2027-03-09 10:00 AM PT. I'll send a calendar invite after you confirm.
- If legal needs specific clause language, Lauren will push a tracked-change SOW draft to the Drive link.

Links: https://app.hubspot.com/deal/54321 | Drive: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1Xk_ExampleLink

Thanks — talk Tuesday?

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Lead, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sarah Li <sarah.li@datahealth.com>, procurement@datahealth.com
Cc: 
Date: Tue, 02 Mar 2027 08:17:00 -0700
Subject: Draft MSA & Order Form (incl. DPA) - initial delivery

Sarah / DataHealth procurement team —

Attaching the first draft of the MSA + Order Form and a companion DPA for your review (Redlined_MSA_OrderForm_DPA_v1.docx). Key items we expect you may flag: SCCs selection, processor/controller role language, and any transfer impact specifics for EU→US processing.

Ask / next steps:
- Can you confirm who on your side will own the legal redlines (in-house GC vs outside counsel) and a target 5-business-day review window?
- Please call out any substantive SCC deviation requests or requests to convert processor duties to controller-led obligations.
- If you have a preferred transfer-mitigation template (TIA/contractual addendum), share it and we’ll map it to our exports and subprocessors.

Attachments: Redlined_MSA_OrderForm_DPA_v1.docx (redline)

Thanks — happy to hop on a quick sync this week if helpful.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com
(415) 555-0124

Redwood Inference — production-first inference for developers


From: Sarah Li <sarah.li@datahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sara Nguyen <sara.nguyen@datahealth.com>, procurement@datahealth.com
Date: Tue, 02 Mar 2027 11:09:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Draft MSA & Order Form (incl. DPA) - initial delivery

Ben —

Thanks for sending. I’m DataHealth’s lead on contracting for this engagement; copying Sara (outside counsel) and procurement. We’ve reviewed the package at a high level and have the DPA redline attached (DataHealth_DPA_Redlines.pdf). High-level items:

- SCCs: we request Module 2 + 3 (customer-controlled additions) rather than your proposed Controller→Processor baseline. We need to understand Redwood’s positions on subprocessors and whether any processing will occur in the US controlled by third parties.
- Roles: we’ve proposed edits that shift several obligations toward your side (processor responsibilities). Please confirm whether you can accept processor obligations for deletion requests, data subject access request support, and export controls evidence.
- Transfer impact: because we have regulated EU data, our compliance folks will expect a TIA with mitigations for US transfers (e.g., additional contractual safeguards + technical measures).

We’ve noted a few redlines that are more substantive — Sara and I would like to schedule a 30-minute call with your legal to go through the redlines and a proposed TIA structure. Availability tomorrow or Thursday afternoon (GMT).

Attachments: DataHealth_DPA_Redlines.pdf

Regards,
Sarah Li
Director, Vendor Risk & Privacy
DataHealth
sarah.li@datahealth.com

Laura / Karthik / Kimberly —

Forwarding DataHealth’s DPA redlines and the ask to do a short run-through of SCCs + transfer impact mitigations. Core asks from DataHealth: they want stricter SCC selection and want us to accept more processor obligations (DSAR handling, deletion confirmation). They’ve also requested a transfer impact addendum.

Specific points I’d like our input on:
- Which of the DataHealth redlines are deal-breakers vs. negotiable?
- Practical evidence we can provide for EU→US mitigations (SOC 2, subprocessors list, encryption-at-rest details).

> To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
> Cc: Sara Nguyen <sara.nguyen@datahealth.com>, procurement@datahealth.com

>

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: 
Date: Tue, 02 Mar 2027 14:21:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Fwd: DataHealth DPA redlines — need legal / privacy input

Thanks Ben — quick read-through and thoughts below. I attached an internal redline with suggested compromise positions (redline_suggestions_bennett.docx).

Summary positions (initial):
- SCCs: We can accept the standard EU SCCs for Controller→Processor relationships but won’t agree to language that converts Redwood to a Controller for the broader service. We should push back on any clause that transfers liability for customer data governance to Redwood, but we can take on operational obligations (e.g., timely DSAR support, deletion confirmation within agreed SLA).
- DSARs / deletion confirmations: propose the following compromise — Redwood will provide assistance and technical support to honor DSARs; where action requires customer direction (e.g., customer-managed exports) we will comply on receipt of authenticated instructions.
- Subprocessors: provide the current subprocessors list and a 10-business-day notice for additions; propose an exceptions process for emergency engagements.
- Transfer impact addendum (TIA): we should offer a short, pragmatic TIA that documents our controls (SOC 2 link, encryption, access controls) and lists residual risks. We can accept a modestly stronger contractual clause if paired with clear evidence and a fall-back of appropriate audit rights.

Evidence we can share now: SOC 2 report (read-only link), current subprocessor registry, high-level network diagram showing EU processing boundaries. Karthik — can you confirm whether any tokenization/embedding cache copies are persisted in US regions? That affects the mitigation language.

I’ll draft suggested language for the TIA and an annotated SCC mapping. If we can get DataHealth’s list of EU processing categories (PII v. special categories) that would help scope the TIA.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: 
Date: Wed, 03 Mar 2027 09:07:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Fwd: DataHealth DPA redlines — need legal / privacy input

Laura / team —

Quick technical confirmation to inform the TIA draft:
- Processing footprint: primary tokenization and KV cache live in eu-west-1 for EU-region customers. Some asynchronous model fine-tuning telemetry is processed in a secure US environment for model ops — that telemetry is pseudonymized and stored with strong access controls.
- Subprocessors that could touch exported data: logging & metrics vendor (LogsCo), CDN (FastEdge), model hosting infra (VendorX). I’ve attached the processor roles mapping as a CSV (Processor-roles-mapping.csv) in case you want to map obligations to each vendor.
- Mitigations: we rely on encryption in transit and at rest, strict IAM, and a data segregation layer at the tenant boundary. For TIA language, recommend: (i) documented subprocessors + notice, (ii) contractual audit rights for high-risk categories, (iii) encryption + limited access in US-based ops.

I’ll pull the exact telemetry retention numbers and confirm whether any raw EU customer PII ever persists in US backups — that is a likely deal point.

Karthik Iyer
Privacy & Compliance, Redwood Inference


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sarah Li <sarah.li@datahealth.com>, Sara Nguyen <sara.nguyen@datahealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwoodinference.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwoodinference.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 04 Mar 2027 15:43:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Draft MSA & Order Form (incl. DPA) - consolidated response and next steps

Thanks Ben — quick read-through and thoughts below. I attached an internal redline with suggested compromise positions (redline_suggestions_bennett.docx).

Summary positions (initial):
- SCCs: We can accept the standard EU SCCs for Controller→Processor relationships but won’t agree to language that converts Redwood to a Controller for the broader service. We should push back on any clause that transfers liability for customer data governance to Redwood, but we can take on operational obligations (e.g., timely DSAR support, deletion confirmation within agreed SLA).
- DSARs / deletion confirmations: propose the following compromise — Redwood will provide assistance and technical support to honor DSARs; where action requires customer direction (e.g., customer-managed exports) we will comply on receipt of authenticated instructions.
- Subprocessors: provide the current subprocessors list and a 10-business-day notice for additions; propose an exceptions process for emergency engagements.
- Transfer impact addendum (TIA): we should offer a short, pragmatic TIA that documents our controls (SOC 2 link, encryption, access controls) and lists residual risks. We can accept a modestly stronger contractual clause if paired with clear evidence and a fall-back of appropriate audit rights.

Evidence we can share now: SOC 2 report (read-only link), current subprocessor registry, high-level network diagram showing EU processing boundaries. Karthik — can you confirm whether any tokenization/embedding cache copies are persisted in US regions? That affects the mitigation language.

I’ll draft suggested language for the TIA and an annotated SCC mapping. If we can get DataHealth’s list of EU processing categories (PII v. special categories) that would help scope the TIA.

Laura Bennett

Quick technical confirmation to inform the TIA draft:
- Processing footprint: primary tokenization and KV cache live in eu-west-1 for EU-region customers. Some asynchronous model fine-tuning telemetry is processed in a secure US environment for model ops — that telemetry is pseudonymized and stored with strong access controls.
- Subprocessors that could touch exported data: logging & metrics vendor (LogsCo), CDN (FastEdge), model hosting infra (VendorX). I’ve attached the processor roles mapping as a CSV (Processor-roles-mapping.csv) in case you want to map obligations to each vendor.
- Mitigations: we rely on encryption in transit and at rest, strict IAM, and a data segregation layer at the tenant boundary. For TIA language, recommend: (i) documented subprocessors + notice, (ii) contractual audit rights for high-risk categories, (iii) encryption + limited access in US-based ops.

I’ll pull the exact telemetry retention numbers and confirm whether any raw EU customer PII ever persists in US backups — that is a likely deal point.

Subject: Day-of crash plan & strict timebox for guest demos

Hey team,

We landed Olivia for the FinReview webinar on Mar 10 and she wants a hard 10-minute demo window (incl Q&A). Because past guest demos have run long, I want a compact, operational "crash plan" we can hand the host and AV. Goal: shave drift, guarantee transitions, and have escalation paths for technical failure.

Can each of you confirm the following by EOD today: 1) single-line day-of host script that enforces the 10-min timebox, 2) primary and backup AV contact with mobile, 3) the one-sentence fallback we read in case the demo dies, and 4) whether we can shave 90s off the handoff without cutting critical content.

I've attached Olivia's bio and a provisional timing grid. I can draft the host script if you want.

Thanks,
Ben
Senior Field Marketing Manager
Redwood Inference

- AV primary: Marco Ruiz (Streamline AV) — +1 (415) 555-0189, ops@streamline-av.com

- Fallback line: \"We’ll move to a screen-share recording and continue with the discussion in Q&A while AV resolves the issue.\" Short and we can prepend to host script.

If start is 10:00 PT and Olivia has slides, here's a proposed 10-min breakdown (works to the second):
- 0:00–0:30 host intro (30s)
- 0:30–1:10 quick sponsor mention (40s)
- 1:10–1:30 transition to guest (20s)
- 1:30–6:30 guest demo (5:00)
- 6:30–8:30 live Q&A (2:00)
- 8:30–9:30 closing takeaway and CTA (1:00)
- 9:30–10:00 hard stop and handback to host (30s)

That gives the guest 5 minutes demo + 2 minutes Q&A; if Olivia needs the full 10 minutes for demo we flip Q&A to chat-only and surface top questions post-webinar.

I'll push the host script and a \"read if demo fails\" two-line fallback in the next mail.

title: Redwood x BrightOps — co-sell registration and fee split proposal
preview:
From: Alex Murphy <alex@brightops.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-03-02T14:05:00-07:00
Subject: Redwood x BrightOps — reseller structure?

Hi Camila,

Thanks for taking a few minutes with us at the conference last week — really enjoyed the demo and the latency/cost numbers you showed. BrightOps is interested in building a formal resale/co-sell arrangement so we can bring Redwood to our consulting and implementation customers. A couple of initial questions to scope feasibility: 

- Typical rev-share range you offer for ISV resale vs. referral? Any volume tiers?
- Lead registration window (how long after we intro before it’s claimable)?
- Do you require deal registration exclusivity for a territory or customer list?
- Is MDF or marketing credit available for certified partners?

I attached a short outline of BrightOps partner motion so you have context (brightops-partner-outline.pdf). Can we book 30 minutes to run through yields and thresholds? I’m cc’ing Sarah (partnerships) who will join the call.

Best,
Alex Murphy
Head of Alliances
BrightOps Inc.

Attachments: brightops-partner-outline.pdf


From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Murphy <alex@brightops.com>
Cc: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@brightops.com>
Date: 2027-03-02T16:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Redwood x BrightOps — reseller structure?

Hi Alex / Sarah,

Really appreciate the follow-up. BrightOps’ partner profile looks like a good fit. High-level answers up front, then a few asks so we can put a term sheet together: 

- Typical commission: referral credits start at 10% ARR; reseller/co-sell tiers are 15-25% first year depending on seat/commit level.
- Lead registration window: we currently use a 180-day registration window as our baseline.
- Exclusivity: we do not offer broad exclusivity by default; carve-outs for NAM accounts > $X ARR can be discussed on a case-by-case basis.
- MDF: available for approved certification partners; baseline co-invest is 50/50 and scales with joint pipeline.

To move fast I’ll loop in our Sales Ops and Partner GTM folks — Ben Carter (sales ops) and Karthik Iyer (partner GTM) — so they can confirm operational flows (deal registration form, required evidence, and payout timing).

Can you send a few recent deal examples and expected ARR bands so we can map the right commission tier? I’ll draft a concise channel term sheet and share. Proposed times for a quick call: tomorrow 10:30–11:00 PT or 3:00–3:30 PT.

Thanks,
Camila Reyes
Head of Partnerships
Redwood Inference

---
Camila Reyes
Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.ai


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alex Murphy <alex@brightops.com>, Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@brightops.com>
Date: 2027-03-03T09:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Redwood x BrightOps — reseller structure?

Looping in operational details. Quick notes from Sales Ops perspective: 

- Registration qualification: intro must include a first meeting summary + customer email domain and SM contact; marketing-sourced leads require MQL evidence.
- Credit calculation: we pay on recognized ARR at contract signature; commissions paid quarterly with 30-day payout after invoice clears.
- Clawbacks: standard 12-month linear clawback for cancellations.
- For co-sell deals where BrightOps provides implementation services, we can add a technical services uplift (additional 5% to reseller split) if SI delivers certified Redwood implementation.

I’ve drafted an initial term sheet (redwood-brightops-channel-term-sheet-v1.pdf) which sets a starting band: 18% first year for qualified reseller-sourced deals, 12% for referral-introduced closed deals. Also included lead registration form flow and sample DOCS.

Attaching term sheet for review. Camila — if you’re happy I’ll seed this into DocuSign and include a short MSA addendum for channel partners.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Manager, Sales Operations

Cc: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@brightops.com>
